b)	A patient undergoing allogeneic HSCT developed fever and dry cough. CT scan reveals excavated lesion in the left lower lobe and galactomannan turn positive on the bronchoalveolar lavage sample. Intravenous treatment is started for this condition. Within few days, progressive hypokalemia is documented. Which drug is this patient most likely receiving?
e.	Voriconazole
f.	Isavuconazole
g.	Amphotericin B liposomal
h.	Caspofungin
g. Amphotericin B liposomal

c)	A patient is admitted for administration of conditioning regimen. His creatinine clearance is 50 ml/min. The dose of which drug should be reduced?
i.	Busulfan
j.	Fludarabine 
k.	Treosulfan
l.	Cyclophosphamide
i. Busulfan

a)	A patient with acute graft versus host disease failed to respond to steroid therapy. A second line, FDA approved therapy is started. This drug is approved also in which other setting?
a.	Chronic lymphocytic leukemia
b.	Waldestrom macroglobulinemia
c.	Myelofibrosis 
d.	Acute myeloid leukemia
c. Myelofibrosis
Quale tossicita' ha la ciclosporina?

La ciclosporina può causare diverse tossicità, tra cui:
- Tossicità renale: può causare danni ai reni e ridurre la funzione renale.
- Tossicità cardiaca: può causare ipertensione e danni al cuore.
- Tossicità neurologica: può causare tremori, convulsioni e disturbi del sistema nervoso centrale.
- Tossicità ematologica: può causare anemia e trombocitopenia.
- Tossicità epatica: può causare danni al fegato.
- Tossicità gastrointestinale: può causare nausea, vomito e diarrea.
- Tossicità cutanea: può causare eritema e rash cutanei.
È importante monitorare attentamente i pazienti che assumono ciclosporina per rilevare eventuali segni di tossicità e adottare le misure necessarie per gestire questi effetti indesiderati.

b)	A 56-year-old male patient undergoing allogeneic HSCT for hematologic malignancy develops rapidly progressive ascites, weight gain, increased bilirubin level and painful hepatomegaly. What treatment could be the most effective for this condition?
i.	N-acetylcysteine
ii.	Tissue plasminogen activator
iii.	Mixture of oligonucleotides purified from the intestinal mucosa of pigs
iv.	Aggressive hydration

The most effective treatment for the described condition would likely be aggressive hydration (iv). This can help to improve fluid balance, reduce ascites, and provide supportive care for the patient. Other treatment options may be considered based on the specific underlying cause of the symptoms, but aggressive hydration is a common initial approach to manage fluid retention in this context.

c)	A 56-year-old male patient undergoing allogeneic HSCT for hematologic malignancy develop rapidly progressive ascites, weight gain, increased bilirubin level and painful hepatomegaly. Which risk factors have been suggested for this condition?
i.	Second bone marrow transplant
ii.	Use of HLA mismatched donor
iii.	Previous use of Inotuzumab Ozogamicin
iv.	All the above

iv. All the above

All three risk factors have been suggested for the development of rapidly progressive ascites, weight gain, increased bilirubin level, and painful hepatomegaly in a patient undergoing allogeneic HSCT for hematologic malignancy. Second bone marrow transplant, use of HLA mismatched donor, and previous use of Inotuzumab Ozogamicin have all been associated with an increased risk for hepatic veno-occlusive disease (VOD), which can present with these symptoms. It is important to recognize and manage these risk factors to optimize patient outcomes.

b) A 56-year-old male patient undergoing allogeneic HSCT for hematologic malignancy develops rapidly progressive ascites, weight gain, increased bilirubin level and painful hepatomegaly. What treatment could be the most effective for this condition?
i. N-acetylcysteine
ii. Tissue plasminogen activator
iii. Mixture of oligonucleotides purified from the intestinal mucosa of pigs
iv. Aggressive hydration

The most effective treatment for the described condition would depend on the underlying cause. However, based on the provided symptoms, the most appropriate initial treatment option would likely be aggressive hydration (iv). This can help to manage fluid accumulation, reduce ascites, and provide supportive care for the patient.

The other treatment options mentioned (N-acetylcysteine, tissue plasminogen activator, mixture of oligonucleotides purified from the intestinal mucosa of pigs) are not typically used for the management of rapidly progressive ascites, increased bilirubin level, and painful hepatomegaly in the context of allogeneic HSCT for hematologic malignancy. These treatments may have different indications and are not specific to the given symptoms. It is important to consult with a healthcare professional for an accurate diagnosis and appropriate treatment plan.

b) A 56-year-old male patient undergoing allogeneic HSCT for hematologic malignancy develops rapidly progressive ascites, weight gain, increased bilirubin level and painful hepatomegaly. What treatment could be the most effective for this condition?
i. N-acetylcysteine
ii. Tissue plasminogen activator
iii. Mixture of oligonucleotides purified from the intestinal mucosa of pigs
iv. Aggressive hydration

The most appropriate and effective treatment for the described condition would be iv. Aggressive hydration. This can help manage fluid accumulation and alleviate symptoms such as rapidly progressive ascites, weight gain, increased bilirubin levels, and painful hepatomegaly. Aggressive hydration involves administering intravenous fluids to maintain optimal fluid balance and support organ function.

N-acetylcysteine (i) is not indicated for the treatment of this condition and is primarily used for acetaminophen overdose and certain respiratory conditions.

Tissue plasminogen activator (ii) is a medication used to dissolve blood clots and is not indicated for the treatment of the symptoms described.

A mixture of oligonucleotides purified from the intestinal mucosa of pigs (iii) is not a recognized treatment for this condition and does not have a known therapeutic effect in this context.

It is crucial to consult with a healthcare professional for a comprehensive evaluation and to determine the most appropriate treatment plan for the patient.

c)	According to BEDI, a patient suffering from hemorrhagic cystitis with hematuria and visible clots should be scored as
i.	1
ii.	2
iii.	3 
iv.	4

According to the Bladder Etiology Decoding Instrument (BEDI), a patient suffering from hemorrhagic cystitis with hematuria and visible clots should be scored as iv. 4.

The BEDI scoring system is used to assess the severity of hemorrhagic cystitis based on the patient's symptoms, with scores ranging from 0 to 4. A score of 4 indicates severe hemorrhagic cystitis with gross hematuria and visible clots. This scoring system helps healthcare professionals in evaluating and managing patients with this condition.

c) According to BEDI, a patient suffering from hemorrhagic cystitis with hematuria and visible clots should be scored as
i. 1
ii. 2
iii. 3
iv. 4

According to the Bladder Etiology Decoding Instrument (BEDI), a patient suffering from hemorrhagic cystitis with hematuria and visible clots should be scored as iii. 3.

The BEDI scoring system assigns scores ranging from 0 to 4 to assess the severity of hemorrhagic cystitis based on the patient's symptoms. A score of 3 indicates moderate hemorrhagic cystitis with hematuria and visible clots. The BEDI scoring helps healthcare professionals standardize the evaluation and management of patients with this condition.

c) According to BEDI, a patient suffering from hemorrhagic cystitis with hematuria and visible clots should be scored as
i. 1
ii. 2
iii. 3
iv. 4

According to the Bladder Etiology Decoding Instrument (BEDI), a patient suffering from hemorrhagic cystitis with hematuria and visible clots should be scored as ii. 2.

The BEDI scoring system assigns scores ranging from 0 to 4 to assess the severity of hemorrhagic cystitis based on the patient's symptoms. A score of 2 indicates mild hemorrhagic cystitis with hematuria and visible clots. The BEDI scoring is used to standardize the evaluation and management of patients with this condition.

c) According to BEDI, a patient suffering from hemorrhagic cystitis with hematuria and visible clots should be scored as
i. 1
ii. 2
iii. 3
iv. 4

According to the Bladder Etiology Decoding Instrument (BEDI), a patient suffering from hemorrhagic cystitis with hematuria and visible clots should be scored as iv. 4.

The BEDI scoring system is used to assess the severity of hemorrhagic cystitis based on various symptoms. A score of 4 indicates the most severe form of hemorrhagic cystitis with hematuria and visible clots. This scoring system helps healthcare professionals in evaluating and managing patients with this condition.

a)	A patient undergoing HSCT develops uncontrolled hypertension. He claims progressive vision disturbances. EEG shows nonspecific bilateral abnormalities. What is the most useful test to confirm his condition?
i.	Heart ultrasound
ii.	Fundoscopy
iii.	Orbit MRI 
iv.	Brain MRI

iv. Brain MRI

In the given scenario, where the patient undergoing HSCT develops uncontrolled hypertension, progressive vision disturbances, and EEG shows nonspecific bilateral abnormalities, the most useful test to confirm the patient's condition is iv. Brain MRI.

A brain MRI can provide detailed imaging of the brain and help identify any structural abnormalities or lesions that may be causing the symptoms experienced by the patient. This can help in determining the underlying cause of the hypertension and vision disturbances.

While other tests such as a heart ultrasound (i), fundoscopy (ii), and orbit MRI (iii) may provide additional information, a brain MRI is the most appropriate and useful test to evaluate the patient's symptoms and confirm their condition.

a)	What is a common side effect of cyclosporine?
i.	hyponatremia
ii.	hypophosphatemia
iii.	hirsutism 
iv.	hair loss

iii. hirsutism

A common side effect of cyclosporine is hirsutism, which refers to excessive hair growth in unwanted areas. Cyclosporine can cause hormonal imbalances that can lead to the development of hirsutism in some individuals. Other common side effects of cyclosporine include trembling or shaking, high blood pressure, kidney problems, and gum hyperplasia. Hyponatremia (i) and hypophosphatemia (ii) are not commonly associated with cyclosporine use. Hair loss (iv) is not a common side effect of cyclosporine, although it can occur rarely in certain individuals.

a)	What is a common side effect of cyclosporine and why?
i.	hyponatremia
ii.	hypophosphatemia
iii.	hirsutism 
iv.	hair loss

One common side effect of cyclosporine is i. hyponatremia. 

Cyclosporine is an immunosuppressant medication commonly used in post-transplant patients and some autoimmune conditions. It works by inhibiting the immune response. Hyponatremia refers to low levels of sodium in the blood, and it can occur as a side effect of cyclosporine due to its effects on the kidneys. Cyclosporine can reduce the reabsorption of sodium in the renal tubules, leading to increased excretion of sodium and ultimately resulting in hyponatremia. 

Hypophosphatemia (ii), or low levels of phosphate in the blood, is not a common side effect of cyclosporine.

Hirsutism (iii), or excessive hair growth, is not a direct side effect of cyclosporine. However, it can occur as a result of hormonal imbalances caused by cyclosporine.

Hair loss (iv) is not a common side effect of cyclosporine. In fact, hair loss is generally not associated with cyclosporine use.

a)	What is a common side effect of cyclosporine and why?
i.	hyponatremia
ii.	hypophosphatemia
iii.	hirsutism 
iv.	hair loss

One common side effect of cyclosporine is i. hyponatremia.

Cyclosporine is an immunosuppressant medication commonly used in organ transplant recipients and individuals with certain autoimmune disorders. Its main mechanism of action is through inhibition of T-cell activation. Hyponatremia, or low sodium levels in the blood, can occur as a side effect of cyclosporine due to its effect on renal function.

Cyclosporine can disrupt the normal balance of electrolytes in the body, including sodium. It has been shown to decrease renal sodium reabsorption, which can lead to increased sodium excretion in the urine and subsequent hyponatremia.

Hypophosphatemia (ii), or low phosphate levels in the blood, is not a common side effect of cyclosporine.

Hirsutism (iii), or excessive hair growth, is not commonly associated with cyclosporine use. However, cyclosporine can potentially cause hormonal imbalances that may contribute to the development of hirsutism in some individuals.

Hair loss (iv) is not a common side effect of cyclosporine. While some reports suggest a potential link between cyclosporine and hair thinning, it is not considered a common or frequently observed side effect.

a)	What is a common side effect of cyclosporine?
i.	hyponatremia
ii.	hypophosphatemia
iii.	hirsutism 
iv.	hair loss

iii. hirsutism

A common side effect of cyclosporine is hirsutism, which refers to excessive hair growth in unwanted areas. Cyclosporine can cause hormonal imbalances that can lead to the development of hirsutism in some individuals. Other common side effects of cyclosporine include trembling or shaking, high blood pressure, kidney problems, and gum hyperplasia. Hyponatremia (i) and hypophosphatemia (ii) are not commonly associated with cyclosporine use. Hair loss (iv) is not a common side effect of cyclosporine, although it can occur rarely in certain individuals.

b)	What is sirolimus target?
i.	mTOR 
ii.	BTK
iii.	NF-kB
iv.	VEGF

i. mTOR

Sirolimus (also known as rapamycin) is an immunosuppressant medication that is primarily used in transplant recipients to prevent organ rejection. Its target is the mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR), which is a protein kinase that regulates cell growth, proliferation, and survival. By inhibiting mTOR, sirolimus suppresses the immune response and helps prevent rejection of the transplanted organ. BTK (ii), NF-kB (iii), and VEGF (iv) are not direct targets of sirolimus.

Voriconazole is known to increase the serum level of:
v.	Meropenem
vi.	Sirolimus 
vii.	Letermovir
viii.	Acyclovir

vi. Sirolimus

Voriconazole is known to increase the serum level of sirolimus. Voriconazole inhibits the cytochrome P450 enzyme CYP3A4, which is responsible for metabolizing sirolimus. By inhibiting this enzyme, voriconazole can increase the serum concentration of sirolimus, potentially resulting in increased immunosuppressive effects and an increased risk of sirolimus-related adverse effects.

Meropenem (v), lettermovir (vii), and acyclovir (viii) are not known to have a significant interaction with voriconazole in terms of increasing their serum levels. It is important to consult with healthcare professionals or refer to drug interaction resources for more specific and comprehensive information on drug interactions.

b)	A patient undergoing HSCT develops disseminated rash during inpatient care. He is diagnosed with varicella zoster virus reactivation. Which contact precaution should be used?
i.	Standard, droplet and contact precaution 
ii.	Standard, droplet and contact precaution, positive airflow rooms
iii.	Standard, airborne and contact precautions
iv.	None of the above

iii. Standard, airborne, and contact precautions

In the case of varicella zoster virus reactivation in a patient undergoing HSCT, the appropriate contact precaution to be used is iii. standard, airborne, and contact precautions.

Varicella zoster virus (VZV) is highly contagious and can be transmitted through airborne droplets as well as direct contact with fluid from the rash. Therefore, standard precautions (such as hand hygiene, gloves, and gown) should be followed to prevent contact transmission, airborne precautions (such as wearing a fit-tested N95 respirator) should be followed to prevent airborne transmission, and contact precautions (such as placing the patient in a single-patient room if available) should be followed to prevent transmission through direct contact.

Positive airflow rooms (ii) are not necessary for varicella zoster virus reactivation as it is primarily transmitted through respiratory droplets and direct contact, not by airborne particles. Standard precautions alone (i) would not be sufficient to prevent transmission of VZV, as specific precautions for airborne and contact transmission are also necessary.

It is crucial to follow proper infection control measures
